ViTs can be categorized into two clusters, one of which includes ViTs with local attention. Local attention is achieved by restricting the attention window size, sharing attention queries, or using low rank matrices to represent attention queries/keys.

The other cluster of ViTs includes ViTs with kernel-based linear attention, where the non-linearity softmax function is approximated by separate kernel embeddings. This approach enables a change in the matrix computation order for reduced computational complexity.

node_2: lack of capabilities to capture global or local context
edge: Despite the promise in alleviating complexity and inference runtime, local attention compromises ViTs' performance due to the lack of capabilities to capture global or local context.
Kernel-based linear attention aims to reduce computational complexity and inference runtime compared to local attention methods.

node_2: high-order residuals at the early training stage
edge: The latter strategy involves using powerful yet costly softmax-based masked attention to approximate high-order residuals at the early training stage.
Existing linear attention modules still underperform compared to expectations, presenting a challenge in achieving the desired balance in ViTs training.
Existing linear attention modules are compared to their vanilla softmax-based counterparts, highlighting the need for better linear attention for improved performance.
Angular kernels are compared to softmax-based attentions in terms of similarity measurements, showing that they perform equally. However, they still suffer from quadratic complexity.
Angular kernels can be divided into linear terms and high-order residuals, indicating a potential connection between the two concepts.
The challenge discussed is how to construct ViTs using only the linear terms, showcasing a relationship between ViTs and linear terms.
Trained ViTs should primarily rely on linear terms towards the end of training, leading to the need for approximating high-order residuals, emphasizing the importance of this relationship.
Costly but powerful modules are required to approximate high-order residuals during training without incurring extra inference costs, highlighting the challenge faced in the context.
Castling-ViT is a framework proposed to address the challenges mentioned in ViTs, impacting the training and inference processes of ViTs.

The framework proposes training ViTs using both types of attention mechanisms but switching to only linear-angular attentions during inference to reduce computational costs, showcasing the relationship between these attention mechanisms.
A new linear-angular attention is developed to close the accuracy gap between linear attention and softmax-based attention, indicating a link between these concepts.
Angular kernels are used to leverage angular attention while keeping linear terms intact, suggesting a connection between angular kernels and linear terms.
Castling-ViT uses two parameterized modules, including a depthwise convolution and an auxiliary masked softmax-based attention, to approximate the high-order residuals

Linear attention works are proposed as efficient alternatives to the costly self-attention module used in ViT architectures.

Kernel-based linear attention is a common design that approximates the softmax function or the full self-attention matrix with orthogonal features or kernel embeddings, allowing a change in computation order. Despite its decent performance, kernel-based linear attention generally underperforms compared to softmax-based attention.
Kernel-based linear attention underperforms compared to softmax-based attention, despite efforts to improve its performance through decomposition and computation order changes.
Kernel-based linear attention is related to ViTs through the mention of efficient ViT variants being supplied in the Appendix, showcasing its use as the backbones for downstream tasks.
Self-attention is a core component of the Transformer model, consisting of multiple heads to capture global-context information.
The Transformer consists of multiple heads, each capturing global-context information by measuring pairwise correlations among all tokens.
Each head's attention matrix H captures information related to the query, key, and value vectors obtained by linearly projecting the input.
The attention head first computes the inner product between the query-key pairs to obtain attention scores.
The attention head computes the inner product between the query and key vectors and then computes a weighted sum over all value vectors.
Outputs from all attention heads are concatenated together before the final linear projection with learnable weights.
The core idea of linear attention is to decompose the similarity measurement function into separate kernel embeddings for efficient computation.
Kernel embeddings allow changing the computation order based on the associative property of matrix multiplication for efficient attention complexity.
The attention complexity is quadratic to the feature dimension d.
One straightforward implementation of linear attention is to use Gaussian RBF kernels to measure the similarity.

The proposed Castling-ViT uses a novel kernel-based linear-angular attention from the spectral angle perspective.

The angular kernel is proposed as an alternative to existing softmax-based attentions, and it involves measuring spectral similarity, taking into consideration the nature of spectral characteristics. The spectral angle, defined as the arccosine of the inner product of two vectors divided by the product of their Euclidean distances, is used as a distance in this context.
The angular kernel is used as a similarity measurement function between the queries Q and keys K, indicating a relationship in the context of attention design.
The spectral angle is used as a distance measurement function between the queries Q and keys K, indicating a relationship in the context of attention design.

The angular kernel is used as a similarity measurement function between queries Q and keys K, where the output range is between 0 and 1. The angular kernel helps in determining the similarity between Qi and Kj based on their alignment and features.

The angular kernel when used in self-attention provides an efficient way to map input data to a high-dimensional feature space where distances and angles are calculated based on a rich feature structure. The angular kernel implicitly maps the input data to a high dimensional feature space after expansion.

Replacing the similarity measurement in self-attention with the angular kernel allows for an efficient mapping of input data to a high-dimensional feature space. The angular kernel is used in self-attention to compute similarities between different data points.

The similarity measurement function is applied to input data to determine the similarity between different data points. The similarity measurement function helps in comparing the features of input data.

The angular kernel maps the input data from the input space to a high or even infinite dimensional feature space. The feature space is where distances and angles are calculated based on a rich feature structure, leading to a more efficient representation of the input data.

The distance between two input features in the feature space is calculated based on the norm of the mapped input data. The distance between two input features is represented as the norm of the mapped input data, which is influenced by the similarity measurement function.
The 2D input space transforms into the 3D feature space after applying the angular kernel.
The square (Euclid) distance and the spectral angle are positively correlated and have a distance range between 0 and 2.
The angles in the input space are correlated to the feature distance after applying the angular kernel.
The question arises on how to incorporate the angular kernel for designing linear attention given its quadratic complexity.
Recalling trigonometric identities leads to the expansion of the arccos function into an infinite series.
The first linear-angular terms can be directly used as the similarity measurement in linear attention.
The higher-order terms of an infinite series introduce a much higher complexity.
A relaxation is proposed to approximate the higher-order terms.

Sparse attention complements linear-angular attention by capturing higher attention scores and potentially supplementing missing higher-order terms.
The relationship between low-rank and sparse approximations is that they complement each other, as shown in recent findings.

node_2: lacks local feature extraction capabilities over its softmax-based counterpart
edge: Linear attention is described as lacking local feature extraction capabilities compared to its softmax-based counterpart, as stated in the context.
In the context of ViT inference, it is desired to reduce or remove softmax-based attentions while keeping the linear-angular attention, known as castling.
The sparsity regularization mentioned leads to softmax attention naturally converging to all zeros during training, according to the context.
With a fixed mask threshold, such as ϵ = 0.02 in image classification experiments, the masks become all zeros, allowing for the removal of the auxiliary branch without impacting model accuracy.
The visualization of the mask-evolving trajectory is mentioned in the context, providing insight into the phenomenon discussed.

Castling-ViT consistently performs better than all baselines across various MACs ranges in terms of the accuracy-efficiency tradeoff.
Castling-LeViT achieves 82.6% top-1 accuracy with only 1.40G MACs while LeViT requires 2.35G FLOPs instead, i.e., ↓40% MACs.
Under comparable MACs, Castling-MViTv2 achieves 84.1% accuracy vs. MViTv2 with 82.3% accuracy instead, i.e., ↑1.8% top-1 accuracy.
Castling-ViT achieves an improved accuracy of ↑0.5% ∼ ↑6.6%, ↑1.0% ∼ ↑8.1%, ↑1.0% ∼ ↑4.1%, and ↑0.6% ∼ ↑2.6% over baselines under <1G MACs, 1∼3G MACs, 3∼10G MACs, and >10G MACs, respectively.
Castling-LeViT/MViTv2 achieves ↓25.7% ∼ ↓55.3% MACs reductions under comparable accuracies or offers a comparable or better accuracy (↑0.6% ∼ ↑1.8%) under comparable MACs.
